topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a closing door. Dr. Albright was leaving. My advisor, the one who had patiently guided me through the labyrinth of my dissertation, was departing. I stared at the screen, the words blurring slightly. My hands, resting on the cool wooden desk, began to tremble, a faint, internal vibration I couldn't suppress. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my cramped dorm room, the sound of my own footsteps echoing too loudly in the silence. Every corner of the room seemed to hold a shadow, a sudden drop in temperature that made the hairs on my arms prickle. I picked up my laptop, intending to check the university portal, but my fingers fumbled, the keys feeling alien beneath my touch. The weight of the upcoming months, suddenly devoid of its familiar anchor, pressed down on my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually going?" I managed, the words tasting dry and brittle in my mouth. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric groaning under my weight, and watched the dust motes dance in the late afternoon sunlight slanting through the window. A strange hollowness opened up in my stomach, a vast, echoing space where routine used to reside. I kept running my thumb over the smooth, cool surface of my textbook cover, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. I found myself staring out at the quad, watching students move with easy confidence, and I felt acutely stranded, like a ship whose captain had just vanished mid-voyage. A tightness constricted my throat; I needed to do something, anything, to fill the void, but my mind refused to offer a coherent plan.

[story 3]

The announcement felt less like news and more like a physical jolt. I walked to the coffee machine, the clatter of the ceramic mugs sounding jarringly loud in the otherwise quiet faculty lounge. I poured the lukewarm coffee, the bitter taste doing little to settle the churning sensation beneath my ribs. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting to see Dr. Albright materialize, only to be met with the empty hallway. My focus fractured; the complex structure of my research suddenly seemed flimsy, the scaffolding I had relied upon dissolving into mist. I began rearranging the stacks of research notes on my desk, pushing papers into illogical configurations, a frantic attempt to impose order on a situation that felt entirely chaotic. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples.
